sn9.14:0.3: Sutta The Thief of a Scent
sn9.14:1.1: I have heard that on one occasion a certain monk was staying among the Kosalans in a forest grove.
sn9.14:1.2: Now at that time, after his meal, returning from his almsround, he went down to a lotus pond and sniffed a red lotus.
sn9.14:1.3: Then the devatā inhabiting the forest grove, feeling sympathy for the monk, desiring his benefit, desiring to bring him to his senses, approached him and addressed him with this verse:
sn9.14:2.2: that hasn’t been given to you.
sn9.14:2.3: This, dear sir, is a factor of stealing.
sn9.14:2.4: You are a thief of a scent.”
sn9.14:3.1: The monk: “I don’t take, don’t damage.
sn9.14:3.2: I sniff at the lotus from far away.
sn9.14:3.3: So why do you call me
sn9.14:4.1: One who digs up the stalks,
sn9.14:4.4: why don’t you say it of him?”
sn9.14:5.3: to him I have nothing to say.
sn9.14:5.4: It’s you to whom I should speak.
sn9.14:6.4: seems as large as a cloud.”
sn9.14:7.1: The monk: “Yes, yakkha, you understand me
sn9.14:7.4: whenever again you see something like this.”
sn9.14:8.1: The devatā: “I don’t depend on you for my living
sn9.14:8.2: nor am I your hired hand.
sn9.14:8.3: You, monk, you yourself should know
sn9.14:8.4: how to go to the good destination.”
sn9.14:9.1: The monk, chastened by the devatā, came to his senses.
